text: The robotic cholecystectomy setup involves inserting a camera port two centimeters above the umbilicus, aligning it with the gallbladder in the right hypochondrium. The patient cart approaches from the right shoulder, with robotic arm one positioned in the left hypochondrium below the left costal margin using an 8mm metallic Da Vinci port with a blunt obturator. Robotic arm two is placed in the right umbilical region or right flank, maintaining at least eight centimeters distance from the camera port to prevent arm clashing. A 5mm trocar is inserted in the right flank as the assistant port.

The assistant retracts the gallbladder fundus towards the right diaphragm using the assistant port. The surgeon utilizes an endorhist hook in robotic arm one and fenestrated bipolar forceps in robotic arm two to manipulate the gallbladder.
The robotic hook is utilized as a right-angle dissector, cutting instrument, and dissecting tool, demonstrating its versatility due to its multiple degrees of freedom.

The surgeon completes the anterior and posterior dissection, creating a window behind the cystic duct. The critical view of safety is achieved, revealing the cystic artery, cystic duct, and liver surface.
The surgeon uses endoristed instruments to dissect the gallbladder to achieve the critical view of safety. A hemolog clip applier is then used to clip and divide the cystic duct and artery.

The surgeon uses routine laparoscopic scissors to divide the cystic duct and artery. The gallbladder is then dissected off with enhanced precision due to the superior three-dimensional vision provided by the robotic system, allowing for detailed visualization of the surgical field.
The gallbladder is placed into an endobag and retrieved through the umbilical trocar. The robot is undocked, and a 5mm scope is inserted through the left hypochondrial trocar for visualization.
